name: Federal (ECPA / Wiretap Act)
notes: Nationwide floor: a party to a conversation — or anyone with one party's prior consent — may record, UNLESS the recording is for a criminal or tortious purpose. States may impose stricter all-party rules; they cannot drop below this floor. Civil liability under 18 U.S.C. § 2520.

name: Alabama
notes: One-party consent — a participant may record their own conversation.

name: Alaska
notes: One-party consent — a participant may record their own conversation.

name: Arizona
notes: One-party consent — a participant may record their own conversation.

name: Arkansas
notes: One-party consent — a participant may record their own conversation.

name: California
notes: All-party consent for 'confidential communications' (those carrying a reasonable expectation of privacy). Kearney held California law can protect a California resident even when the recorder sits in a one-party-consent state — a key reason to default to all-party on cross-state calls.

name: Colorado
notes: One-party consent — a participant may record their own conversation.

name: Connecticut
notes: Mixed. Criminal eavesdropping is effectively one-party, but the civil telephone-recording statute (§ 52-570d) requires all-party consent or notice for phone calls. Treat phone calls as all-party.

name: Delaware
statute: Del. Code Ann. tit. 11, § 1335 (privacy); cf. tit. 11, § 2402 (wiretap, reads one-party)
notes: Conflicting statutes: the wiretap statute reads one-party, but the broader privacy statute (§ 1335) is widely read as all-party. Sources disagree — treat as all-party to be safe.

name: Florida
notes: All-party consent for private communications. An unauthorized interception is generally a third-degree felony (§ 934.03(4)(a)), with a narrow misdemeanor exception for certain non-commercial first offenses involving unencrypted radio (§ 934.03(4)(b)) — not ordinary call/in-person recording.

name: Georgia
notes: One-party consent for AUDIO. But secret VIDEO/visual recording of activities in a private place out of public view requires the consent of all observed persons (§ 16-11-62(2); State v. Cohen) — the one-party rule covers sound, not images.

name: Hawaii
notes: Generally one-party (a participant carrying a device may record), but installing a recording device in a private place requires the consent of those entitled to privacy there. State v. Lee: a participant wearing a device is not an 'installation', so one-party recording stays lawful even in private. Flagged so users verify private-place situations.

name: Idaho
notes: One-party consent — a participant may record their own conversation.

name: Illinois
caseLaw: People v. Clark; People v. Melongo (Ill. 2014)
notes: Bars only the SURREPTITIOUS recording of a PRIVATE conversation without all parties' consent — open or known recording is lawful, and a conversation is 'private' only where a party reasonably expects privacy (phone calls are not automatically private). The pre-2014 'any conversation' statute was struck on First Amendment grounds and rewritten.

name: Indiana
notes: One-party consent. The older citation § 35-33.5-1-5 was repealed and recodified to § 35-31.5-2-176; the operative offense is § 35-33.5-5-5.

name: Iowa
notes: One-party consent — a participant may record their own conversation.

name: Kansas
notes: One-party consent — a participant may record their own conversation.

name: Kentucky
notes: One-party consent — a participant may record their own conversation.

name: Louisiana
notes: One-party consent — a participant may record their own conversation.

name: Maine
notes: One-party for phone; in-person recording in places with a heightened expectation of privacy may require all-party consent. Modeled as mixed to be safe.

name: Maryland
statute: Md. Code Ann., Cts. & Jud. Proc. § 10-402; § 10-401
notes: All-party consent. Telephone (wire) calls always require all parties' prior consent; in-person (oral) communications require all-party consent only where there is a reasonable expectation of privacy (§ 10-401(13)).

name: Massachusetts
notes: Bans SECRET recording rather than requiring affirmative consent: open, announced, or plainly-visible recording is lawful even without express agreement (Commonwealth v. Hyde). Functions as all-party for any hidden device. Has no reasonable-expectation-of-privacy exception, so it is broader than most all-party laws.

name: Michigan
notes: Contested. The eavesdropping statute reads all-party, but Sullivan v. Gray held a PARTICIPANT may record their own conversation without others' consent — functionally one-party for participants. Treat as uncertain and verify locally.

name: Minnesota
notes: One-party consent — a participant may record their own conversation.

name: Mississippi
notes: One-party consent — a participant may record their own conversation.

name: Missouri
notes: One-party for phone; in-person oral communications are best treated as requiring all-party consent. Modeled as mixed to be safe.

name: Montana
notes: Requires all-party KNOWLEDGE, not strict consent: a single audible warning lets recording proceed (§ 45-8-213(2)(a)(iii)), and the offense needs a HIDDEN device. Treated as all-party because covert recording is barred, but note it is really a notification rule.

name: Nebraska
notes: One-party consent — a participant may record their own conversation.

name: Nevada
notes: Mixed. Phone calls require all-party consent by judicial construction (Lane v. Allstate, a 3-2 decision reading § 200.620), while in-person conversations are statutory one-party (§ 200.650). Sources disagree on placement — verify locally.

name: New Hampshire
caseLaw: State v. Clark (2024); State v. Hersom (2025)
notes: All-party consent is the default. Intercepting without all parties' consent is a felony only when willful (§ 570-A:2, I); a party who records their own conversation without others' consent commits a misdemeanor (§ 570-A:2, I-a). Either way it is unlawful — get everyone's consent.

name: New Jersey
notes: One-party consent — a participant may record their own conversation.

name: New Mexico
notes: One-party consent — a participant may record their own conversation.

name: New York
notes: One-party consent — a participant may record their own conversation.

name: North Carolina
notes: One-party consent — a participant may record their own conversation.

name: North Dakota
notes: One-party consent — a participant may record their own conversation.

name: Ohio
notes: One-party consent — a participant may record their own conversation.

name: Oklahoma
notes: One-party consent — a participant may record their own conversation.

name: Oregon
caseLaw: Project Veritas v. Schmidt (9th Cir. en banc 2025)
notes: Mixed. One-party for telephone/electronic communications, but in-person oral conversations require all-party NOTICE (the speaker must be informed). The notice requirement was upheld en banc by the 9th Circuit in 2025.

name: Pennsylvania
notes: All-party consent for communications carrying a reasonable expectation of privacy (Wiretapping and Electronic Surveillance Control Act).

name: Rhode Island
notes: One-party consent — a participant may record their own conversation.

name: South Carolina
notes: One-party consent — a participant may record their own conversation.

name: South Dakota
notes: One-party consent — a participant may record their own conversation.

name: Tennessee
notes: One-party consent — a participant may record their own conversation.

name: Texas
statute: Tex. Penal Code § 16.02; Tex. Civ. Prac. & Rem. Code § 123.001-004
notes: One-party consent — a participant may record their own conversation (criminal and civil statutes align).

name: Utah
notes: One-party consent — a participant may record their own conversation.

name: Vermont
statute: No recording statute; 18 U.S.C. § 2511 applies by default
notes: No state recording statute, so the federal one-party rule applies by default; case law (State v. Geraw) recognizes limited in-home privacy protections.

name: Virginia
notes: One-party consent — a participant may record their own conversation.

name: Washington
notes: All-party consent for private communications. Consent can be satisfied by a clearly recorded announcement at the start of the conversation.

name: West Virginia
notes: One-party consent — a participant may record their own conversation.

name: Wisconsin
notes: One-party consent — a participant may record their own conversation.

name: Wyoming
notes: One-party consent — a participant may record their own conversation.

name: District of Columbia
notes: One-party consent — a participant may record their own conversation.

name: France
notes: Recording the private words of another without consent is a criminal offence — and this applies to a PARTICIPANT, not just an eavesdropper. Effectively all-party for any private conversation.
dataProtection: GDPR — recording is processing of personal data; needs a lawful basis + transparency.

name: Germany
notes: Strong protection of the 'confidentiality of the spoken word': secretly recording the non-public spoken word is criminal and applies to participants. Effectively all-party for private speech.
dataProtection: GDPR / BDSG — recording is processing; needs a lawful basis + transparency.

name: United Kingdom
statute: UK GDPR / Data Protection Act 2018; RIPA/IPA (interception)
notes: No general criminal bar on a participant recording for personal use, so it approximates one-party for individuals. BUT if you process, share, or publish the recording (especially for business), UK GDPR transparency and lawful-basis duties apply, and interception law (RIPA/IPA) governs third-party interception.
dataProtection: UK GDPR / DPA 2018 — ICO-enforced; transparency + lawful basis for any non-personal use.

name: European Union / EEA (GDPR baseline)
notes: Recording a conversation is processing of personal data. Even where no criminal speech-secrecy law applies, GDPR requires a lawful basis, transparency/notice, data minimisation, and retention limits — so explicit all-party consent is the safe baseline across the EU/EEA. Individual member states (e.g. France, Germany) add stricter criminal rules.
dataProtection: GDPR — lawful basis + transparency; large-scale or biometric processing may need a DPIA.

name: Spain
notes: A participant may generally record their own conversation (one-party), but publishing or sharing it can violate the constitutional right to privacy/own image, and GDPR duties apply to any further processing.
dataProtection: GDPR / LOPDGDD — transparency + lawful basis for any non-personal use.

name: Canada
statute: Criminal Code s. 184 (s. 183.1 consent of a party)
notes: One-party consent: a participant may record a private communication they are part of. Federal PIPEDA / provincial privacy laws add duties for organizations collecting/using recordings.
dataProtection: PIPEDA / provincial privacy laws for organizational use.

name: Australia
statute: State/territory Surveillance Devices / Listening Devices Acts; TIA Act 1979 (federal, calls in transit)
notes: Fully state-by-state — roughly half require all-party consent even from a participant (e.g. WA, ACT), while others permit a party to record to protect their lawful interests. Several states also restrict PUBLICATION separately. Treat as uncertain and check the specific state.

name: Japan
statute: No general two-party-consent statute; APPI (data protection)
notes: One-party by legislative silence: secretly recording a conversation you are part of is generally lawful and such recordings are often admissible in court. BUT cultural reticence about privacy and causing 'meiwaku' (trouble/nuisance) is strong — a sharp law-vs-culture gap. Disclose even though the law does not strictly require it.
dataProtection: APPI for organizational handling of recordings.

name: India
statute: No single statute; Indian Telegraph Act; post-Puttaswamy privacy right; DPDP Act 2023
notes: One-party in practice — a participant recording their own conversation is generally accepted, and such recordings are often admitted as evidence. Constitutional privacy (K.S. Puttaswamy) and the DPDP Act 2023 create tension around covert recording and downstream use.

name: Singapore
statute: PDPA (organizations); no general bar on participant recording
notes: De facto one-party for individuals — a participant may record their own conversation. But the PDPA imposes notice/consent duties on ORGANIZATIONS that collect, use, or store recordings, and publication can raise separate issues.

name: Brazil
notes: One-party: the Supreme Federal Court has held a participant may record their own conversation, and such recordings can be valid evidence. The LGPD adds notice/consent and lawful-basis duties for organizations that process the audio.

name: Mexico
notes: One-party: a participant in a private communication may record it (constitutional exception, Art. 16); a non-participant intercepting it cannot. The LFPDPPP adds duties for organizations handling personal data in the recording.
